danger_signals.visual_form: Enclosed second-level areas with distinctive visual boundaries
danger_signals.meaning: Mandatory avoidance mechanic; failure resets entire world progress

danger_signals.visual_form: Third level of each world concludes with boss battle arena
danger_signals.meaning: High-risk combat zone; progression gate requiring victory

danger_signals.visual_form: Final world opponent in Monty's Mansion
danger_signals.meaning: Primary antagonist; kidnapper of Babs Bunny; ultimate threat

danger_signals.visual_form: Varied terrain across six worlds (Hills, Wetlands, Trees, Downtown, Wackyland, Monty's Mansion)

safety_signals.meaning: Enables character switching to gain special abilities (flight, wall destruction, climbing)
Provides extra lives via carrot exchange (30 carrots = 1 life); support mechanism

safety_signals.visual_form: Four playable characters with distinct visual representations
safety_signals.meaning: Tactical safety options: Plucky (flight/swimming), Dizzy (wall destruction), Furrball (climbing), Buster (high jump)

safety_signals.visual_form: Level completion portals in Elmyra avoidance zones

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Buster Bunny protagonist identity; Tiny Toons character roster
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Alternate selectable characters (Dizzy Devil, Furrball, Plucky Duck) with unique visual silhouettes and abilities
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Allied presence via Hamton support; carrot economy for life extension

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Montana Max (Monty) as named kidnapper; Elmyra as chase threat
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Boss encounters per world; Elmyra as recurring capture threat
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Monty's Mansion as final stronghold; Elmyra appears in second-level enclosed zones

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Player starts as Buster Bunny with rescue mission framing
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Initial character is Buster; star ball mechanic enables tactical character switching for world-specific challenges

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Level structure: worlds 1-4 contain 3 levels each; worlds 5-6 contain 1 level each
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Increasing difficulty and spatial compression; final worlds are more condensed encounters

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Third level of each world isolates combat space from platforming challenges
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Forced combat scenario; cannot progress without defeating boss

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enclosed second-level area with distinct visual boundary; enemy presence emphasized
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Pure evasion gameplay; capture = world reset penalty

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Monty's Mansion (single-level world) as final stage
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Montana Max as final boss; Babs Bunny rescue objective completion

environmental_storytelling.example: Six distinct worlds (The Hills, The Wetlands, The Trees, Downtown, Wackyland, Monty's Mansion) each communicate setting through level design and visual presentation
Plucky Duck's superior swimming ability is narrative-meaningful in Wetlands world; Furrball's climbing in Trees world; Dizzy's destruction in cave/wall-heavy zones
Hamton trading carrots for lives establishes safe hub economy; reinforces Toon character relationships
Monty's Mansion as final world signals escalation and boss authority; enclosed Elmyra zones create dramatic pressure sequences
Babs Bunny kidnapping by Montana Max establishes narrative stakes; Buster Bunny as default protagonist reinforces player agency in rescue objective

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Highest jump capability; primary control character; can swap to specialized characters via star ball
Brief flight capability; superior swimming; tactical advantage in aquatic zones
Spin mode destroys walls and most enemies; wall-breaking ability unique to this character
Can climb vertical surfaces; slides down slowly instead of falling; vertical traversal specialist

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Evasion threat in second-level enclosed areas
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Chase mechanic; capture triggers world reset; appears only in bounded zones, not open levels
Final boss in Monty's Mansion; represents primary conflict objective
Provides extra lives via carrot exchange (30 carrots per life); allied presence; non-hostile
Kidnapped by Monty; objective/motivation marker; not directly player-controlled

notes: Tiny Toon Adventures (1991) uses character-driven platformer design where visual identity of characters communicates ability availability and tactical options. The game employs minimal narrative exposition and relies on Looney Tunes IP recognition for world-state communication. Level structure (3-3-3-3-1-1 progression) and Elmyra penalty mechanic create escalating difficulty through spatial and mechanical progression rather than explicit visual world-state shifts. Antagonists (Elmyra, Monty) are communicated through encounter structure and boss encounters rather than faction cosmetics. The star-ball character-swap mechanic is the primary player-agency signal system for navigating varied world challenges.
